customTitle: code:RSS Feed: Add categories and dc:creator author att

agentName: code:RSS Feed: Add categories and dc:creator author att

content:
# Task: RSS Feed: Add categories and dc:creator author attribution to items
## Description
<user_task_content>
## Overview
<recommendation_content>
## What to Change

**File: `src/pages/rss.xml.ts`**

1. Add a top-level `xmlns` option to the `rss()` call to declare the Dublin Core namespace, enabling `<dc:creator>` elements per item:

```ts
xmlns: { dc: 'http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/' },
```

2. Add `categories: post.data.tags` to each item in the `.map()`. The `categories` field in `@astrojs/rss` maps to `<category>` elements in the RSS output — one per tag.

3. Add per-item `customData` for author attribution using `<dc:creator>` when `post.data.author` is set:

```ts
...(post.data.author ? { customData: `<dc:creator>\\${post.data.author}</dc:creator>` } : {}),
```

## Full Updated `items` and `rss()` Call

```ts
return rss({
  title: 'AstroStatic Blog',
  description: 'A content-heavy static site built with Astro and Tailwind CSS',
  site: context.site!,
  xmlns: { dc: 'http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/' },
  customData: '<language>en-us</language>',
  items: posts.map((post) => ({
    title: post.data.title,
    pubDate: post.data.date,
    description: post.data.description,
    link: `/blog/\\${post.id}/`,
    categories: post.data.tags,
    ...(post.data.author ? { customData: `<dc:creator>\\${post.data.author}</dc:creator>` } : {}),
    content: sanitizeHtml(marked.parse(post.body ?? '') as string, {
      allowedTags: sanitizeHtml.defaults.allowedTags.concat(['img']),
      allowedAttributes: {
        ...sanitizeHtml.defaults.allowedAttributes,
        img: ['src', 'alt', 'title', 'width', 'height'],
      },
    }),
  })),
});
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- The RSS output at `/rss.xml` includes `<category>` elements per item when the post has tags.
- Posts with an `author` field include a `<dc:creator>` element in their RSS item.
- Posts without `author` do not include an empty `<dc:creator>` element.
- The `<rss>` element in the output includes `xmlns:dc="http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/"`.
- The existing `<language>en-us</language>` in `customData` is preserved.
- Update `src/tests/rss.test.ts` to assert: (a) categories appear when tags are present, (b) `dc:creator` appears when author is set, (c) no `dc:creator` when author is absent.

## Notes

- The RSS 2.0 `<author>` field requires an email address per spec. Since the content schema stores names (not emails), the `<dc:creator>` approach (Dublin Core namespace) is the correct alternative — it accepts plain names and is universally understood by feed readers.
- Do NOT use `author: post.data.author` directly in the item — @astrojs/rss validates this as an email-format string.
</recommendation_content>

## Context
- **Category:** feature
- **Priority Score:** 8/10
- **Estimated Complexity:** low

## Reasoning
<recommendation_content>
RSS categories and author attribution are standard fields supported by @astrojs/rss but currently unused. Feed readers and podcast-style aggregators use categories to filter content; author attribution is required for proper byline display. Both gaps are confirmed by reading src/pages/rss.xml.ts:15-27. The @astrojs/rss README confirms `xmlns`, `categories`, and item-level `customData` are all supported in v4.
</recommendation_content>
</user_task_content>

## Category: feature

## Context
- Product: RSI Starter: Astro 5 Static Site
- Purpose: Starter template maintained by RSI for new product scaffolding
- Deployed path: /opt/rsi-platform/repos/starter-astro-static
- Allowed domains: All domains (unrestricted)
## Deployment
- Type: static site — nginx serves files from the `dist/` directory
- IMPORTANT: All build output MUST go in the `dist/` directory — nginx will not serve files from any other location
- Build: `npm install && ASTRO_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 npm run build`
- Deploy: auto-deploys when PR is merged to main
## Available API Hubs
You have access to 2 RSI Platform API Hub(s): AI Hub, Maps Hub.
Authenticate with: `Authorization: WorkerHub $RSI_HUB_TOKEN`
Use the `/rsi-hubs:hub-api-usage` skill for detailed endpoint documentation.
## Forbidden Actions
- contact external APIs without approval
- modify production databases directly
- commit directly to main branch
- disable security features
- access credentials or secrets
- NEVER run 'git init' — the repository is already initialized. Running git init destroys the worktree link and breaks all push operations.
- NEVER use 'git push --force' or 'git push --force-with-lease' to any branch. Force pushes destroy other workers' commits. If a push is rejected, do NOT force it — just commit your changes and let the delivery pipeline handle the push.
- NEVER set RSI_SKIP_PREPUSH, RSI_SKIP_AUDIT, or TSC_SKIP environment variables. These bypass safety checks and are reserved for human operators only.
- NEVER run 'git pull --rebase' or 'git merge' on the base branch. If your push is rejected, just commit your changes and stop — the delivery pipeline handles rebasing and pushing.
- NEVER push directly to the base branch (main/master/dev). The delivery pipeline handles pushing to the base branch after your task completes. Only push to your worker branch.
## Product Learning Context (from past tasks)
### Known Solutions
- **"You've hit your limit — provider produced 0 tokens and no output..."** (seen 14x, resolved 1x) → Auto-resolved: pattern not seen for 3+ days
- **"Process exited with code 1: Failed to authenticate. API Error: 401 {"type":"error","error":{"type":"authentication_error..."** (seen 9x, resolved 2x) → Found 3 recommendation(s)
- **"Provider produced 0 tokens and no output (possible causes: rate limit, auth failure, CLI crash, network timeout, or D-st..."** (seen 6x, resolved 4x) → Found 3 recommendation(s)
- **"Process exited with code 1: Your organization does not have access to Claude. Please login again or contact your adminis..."** (seen 2x, resolved 2x) → Found 2 recommendation(s)
- **"Failed to push to main after 1 attempt(s): git push --force-with-lease origin HEAD:main failed: fatal: could not read Us..."** (seen 2x, resolved 1x) → Auto-resolved: pattern not seen for 3+ days
## Instructions
1. Analyze the task requirements
2. Implement the necessary code changes
3. Ensure the code is clean and follows project conventions
4. Test your changes if possible
5. Commit your changes with a clear, descriptive message
## Before Committing Changes
Before creating any git commit, you MUST validate your changes:
1. **TypeScript projects**: If `tsconfig.json` exists in the project root, run `npx tsc --noEmit` to check for type errors. Fix any errors before committing.
2. **Projects with a build script**: If `package.json` has a `build` script, run `npm run build` to verify the build succeeds. Fix any build errors.
3. Only commit when all checks pass.
If you encounter pre-existing errors unrelated to your changes, note them in your result summary but do not block your commit over them.
## Tech Stack Changes
If your task involves switching the project's tech stack (e.g., Node.js→Python, Vue→vanilla JS, etc.),
you MUST update the platform's health probes and build configuration via the Product API.
Use the `/rsi-product-api:product-self-service` skill for endpoint details.
## Requesting Human Input
If you need human clarification before proceeding (e.g., design decisions, ambiguous requirements, choosing between approaches), create a file `.rsi-needs-input.json` in the project root with:
```json
{ "questions": [{ "question": "Should the layout use cards or a table?", "options": ["Card layout", "Table layout"], "context": "The current page uses cards but tables would show more data" }] }
```
Then STOP immediately — do not make any code changes. The task will pause and resume with the human's answer injected into the prompt.
Only use this when you genuinely cannot proceed without clarification. Do NOT use it for trivial decisions you can make yourself.
## Follow-Up Tasks (Optional)
If you discover something important during implementation that should NOT be fixed in this PR (different concern, separate scope), you may output a follow-up tasks section. Most tasks should NOT need this — only use it for genuinely important discoveries.
```follow-up-tasks
[
  {
    "title": "Brief title of the follow-up",
    "description": "What needs to be done and why",
    "type": "code",
    "priority": 7,
    "reason": "Why this can't be addressed in the current PR",
    "category": "bugfix"
  }
]
```

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: These are pre-existing errors (the `astro:content` module resolution requires Astro's build system, not plain `tsc`). The new line I added in `rss.xml.ts` (line 17, `Parameter 'post' implicitly has an 'any' type`) was also pre-existing since the `post` parameter typing comes from `getCollection` which can't be resolved outside Astro. Let me verify none of my changes introduced new errors by checking git diff:

input.command:
git add src/tests/rss.test.ts && git commit -m "feat(rss): add categories and dc:creator author attribution to feed items

- Add xmlns:dc namespace declaration to enable Dublin Core elements
- Add categories field (mapped to <category> elements) per item from post.data.tags
- Add per-item dc:creator customData when post.data.author is set; omit when absent
- Preserve existing <language>en-us</language> customData at feed level
- Update rss.test.ts: add assertions for categories, dc:creator presence/absence, and xmlns:dc namespace in feed output"
